title: Countries by income classification
subtitle: The World Bank's income classification divides countries into four categories based on their gross national income (GNI) per capita. Thresholds between income groups have changed over time.
caption:
Countries by income classification

The World Bank's income classification divides countries into four categories based on their gross national income (GNI) per capita. Thresholds between income groups have changed over time.

This stacked area chart tracks the count of countries within four income tiers from 1987 to 2024, with the vertical axis measuring the number of countries from 0 to 250. The data shows the total number of classified countries rising from roughly 165 in 1987 to over 210 by the mid-1990s, where it remains relatively stable through 2024. The green area at the top, representing high-income countries, expands significantly in the early 1990s and maintains a steady count of roughly 80 nations by the end of the period. Below this, the light green band for upper-middle income countries grows from approximately 30 to nearly 60, showing a consistent upward trajectory. The pink area for lower-middle income countries starts near 50, peaks around 1995 with over 70 nations, and then declines to roughly 55 by 2024. The purple band at the bottom, indicating low-income countries, rises from about 50 to a peak of roughly 65 in the mid-1990s before falling to approximately 25 by 2024. Because countries are grouped based on the income classification for each respective year, group membership can change over time, allowing nations to move between these bands as their economic status shifts. The visual distribution shows a clear transition where the count of low-income and lower-middle-income countries decreases in the later decades, while the count of upper-middle and high-income countries increases or holds steady. The most prominent visible pattern is the sustained growth in the number of countries classified as upper-middle income and high income coinciding with the reduction in the number of countries classified as low income.
